title: Kid's First Toolbox
summary: A small dowel-handled toolbox sized for a child's first set of real hand tools.
description:
A kid's toolbox doesn't need scaling-down math — it needs to be small enough that a young child can carry it without dragging a corner on the ground. This one is a five-piece box: two sides, two shaped ends, and a plywood bottom, with a dowel handle glued straight through the end pieces.

The **shaped ends are the only tricky cut**, and they're forgiving — this is scrap-plywood-bottom, construction-lumber-sides territory, not a fine furniture project. It's meant to survive a toy chest, not a showroom.

Hand it over with a cheap hammer and some finish nails and let the decorating happen before it ever holds a real tool.

tools.note: For the straight crosscuts on the sides and ends.
Shapes the curved top of the end pieces. A coping or scroll saw works too.
Drills the handle holes and drives the assembly.

materials.name: 3/8" plywood, small offcut
materials.note: The bottom panel — 7-1/2" x 21". Most scrap bins have a piece this size.

cutList.note: Shape a curved or angled top corner on each before assembly.

steps.title: Cut the sides and ends
steps.body: Cut the two 1x4 sides to 19-1/2" and the two 1x8 end pieces to 9". Mark and cut a curved or angled top corner on each end piece with a jigsaw — this is what keeps small hands from catching a sharp corner.

steps.title: Drill the handle holes
steps.body: About 1" down from the top of each end piece, drill a hole sized to your dowel rod, straight through both. Drill both ends before gluing anything — it's much easier to keep the holes aligned on flat, separate pieces than after assembly.

steps.title: Cut the bottom panel
steps.body: Cut the plywood bottom to 7-1/2" x 21".

steps.title: Assemble the box
steps.body: Glue the dowel handle into the holes in both end pieces, then glue and nail the end pieces to the two sides. Glue and nail the plywood bottom to the assembled sides and ends.

steps.title: Sand it smooth
steps.body: Sand every edge and corner smooth — this is the step that matters most on a project meant for small hands. Let a kid help with this part; it's hard to mess up.
